timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Handwriting on the Wall

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — “The Fingers of a Human Hand Wrote on the Plaster”

heb.paragraph: "Suddenly the fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote (kəṭab) on the plaster of the wall" (v.5). Belshazzar’s feast: a thousand nobles, drinking from the sacred vessels taken from Jerusalem’s temple. A hand writes. The king’s face turns pale, his knees knock. No wise man can read it. The queen remembers Daniel.

refs.note: Cyrus’s decree fulfilling Jeremiah’s prophecy — the Persian conquest that Daniel 5 narrates from the Babylonian side.
Babylon the great, drunk with the blood of the saints — Revelation’s Babylon draws on the feast imagery of Daniel 5.
In the hand of the LORD is a cup full of foaming wine — the cup of judgment Belshazzar drinks from literally.

notes.note: Belshazzar’s feast. Sacred vessels desecrated. The sacrilege is deliberate and public.
"The fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote on the wall." Terror enters Babylon’s throne room through handwriting.
The queen mother remembers Daniel. Institutional memory saves the day.
Daniel is brought in. He refuses the gifts but agrees to read the writing.

Calvin reads the vessel-desecration as the sin that tips the scales: Nebuchadnezzar’s sins were against people; Belshazzar’s sin is directly against God’s sacred objects. The escalation provokes immediate judgment.
Calvin notes that God uses the simplest means to terrify the mightiest: a hand and some words on a wall. No army, no earthquake — just handwriting.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET notes that archaeological evidence confirms Belshazzar as Nabonidus’s son who acted as co-regent. The "third highest ruler" offer (v.16) makes sense: Nabonidus is first, Belshazzar second, the reward is third.
The NET observes the detail of writing on "the plaster of the wall" — Babylonian palace walls were covered in white gypsum plaster, providing a visible writing surface in lamplight.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Daniel, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the contrast with Nebuchadnezzar is the chapter’s structural key. Nebuchadnezzar was humbled and restored; Belshazzar knew the story (v.22) but repeated the arrogance without the repentance.
Longman notes the queen mother’s role as living memory: she bridges the reigns, remembering Daniel when the current administration has forgotten him.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Daniel, WBC (1989) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the feast with the temple vessels is Babylon’s last supper — a celebration that becomes a funeral. The empire drinks from God’s cups and dies the same night.
Goldingay reads the disembodied hand as the most economical judgment-scene in the Bible: no angel, no voice from heaven, no natural disaster. Just fingers and plaster. God’s minimalism is terrifying.

collins.source: John J. Collins, Daniel, Hermeneia (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Collins identifies the feast as a deliberate act of hubris: the temple vessels are trophies of conquest, and drinking from them celebrates Babylon’s victory over Jerusalem’s God. The sacrilege is political and theological.

Collins notes the literary artistry: the hand writes while the king watches in real-time. The slow revelation — writing, then failed interpretations, then Daniel’s summons — builds maximum dramatic tension.

hist.context: The chapter moves from Nebuchadnezzar (ch.4) to Belshazzar with no transition — the intervening rulers (Evil-Merodach, Neriglissar, Labashi-Marduk) are skipped. Belshazzar was co-regent with his father Nabonidus, who was absent from Babylon. The Nabonidus Chronicle confirms Belshazzar’s role as acting king. The desecration of the temple vessels is deliberate: Nebuchadnezzar stored but never used them; Belshazzar desecrates them at a drunken feast.

ane.similarity: This Babylonian chronicle records that Cyrus conquered Babylon in 539 BCE while King Nabonidus was absent from the city. The chronicle confirms that Babylon fell rapidly without significant battle, matching Daniel 5's depiction of Belshazzar's feast interrupted by sudden conquest.
ane.difference: The chronicle names Nabonidus as king, while Daniel 5 features Belshazzar as the last Babylonian ruler. Historical records show Belshazzar was Nabonidus's son, serving as regent in Babylon during his father's prolonged absence — explaining both his authority and his offer to make Daniel 'third ruler' (after Nabonidus and himself).
ane.significance: The parallel demonstrates that Daniel 5 preserves genuine historical memory of Babylon's fall while focusing on the dramatic scene of judgment. The 'Belshazzar problem' is resolved by recognizing his actual role as co-regent.

heb.paragraph: "MENE, MENE, TEKEL, PARSIN" (v.25). Daniel recounts Nebuchadnezzar’s humbling then rebukes Belshazzar: "You knew all this, yet you have not humbled yourself" (vv.22–23). MENE — numbered. TEKEL — weighed and found wanting. PARSIN — divided and given to Medes and Persians. That very night Belshazzar is slain (v.30). Darius the Mede takes over (v.31).

refs.note: Jeremiah’s prophecy of 70 years of Babylonian dominion — fulfilled at the moment Daniel 5 describes.
"Fallen, fallen is Babylon!" — the double-fallen formula that Daniel 5 narrates.
In one hour your judgment has come — Revelation’s Babylon falls as suddenly as Daniel’s.

notes.note: "You knew all this, yet you have not humbled yourself." Knowledge without humility is condemnation.
Three words, three verdicts. Numbered, weighed, divided. The Parsin/Persia wordplay is deliberate.
"That very night Belshazzar was slain." The swiftest judgment in the OT. No delay, no reprieve.
"Darius the Mede took over the kingdom." The empire changes hands overnight.
Calvin emphasises the aggravating factor: Belshazzar’s sin is worse than Nebuchadnezzar’s because he had the precedent and ignored it. Privilege increases accountability.
Calvin notes the simultaneity: feast and fall on the same night. The cup of celebration becomes the cup of judgment.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET provides the fullest analysis of the wordplay: mənēʾ (mina/numbered), təqēl (shekel/weighed), parsîn (half-minas/divided + Persia). The words function as both economic metaphor and prophetic verdict.

The NET notes the historical difficulty of "Darius the Mede" — no figure by this exact name appears in extra-biblical records. Proposed identifications include Gubaru (governor of Babylon) or Cyrus himself under a throne-name.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Daniel, NIVAC (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the charge against Belshazzar is not ignorance but defiance. He KNEW Nebuchadnezzar’s story and chose arrogance anyway. Known truth that is rejected produces harder judgment.
Longman contrasts the two kings: Nebuchadnezzar was given twelve months to repent (4:29); Belshazzar is given one evening. The window of grace narrows for those who have more precedent to learn from.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Daniel, WBC (1989) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the three words function as a death sentence in commercial language — the empire’s accounts have been audited and found deficient. God as divine accountant.

Goldingay reads the overnight regime change as the book’s most compressed narrative: feast (v.1), writing (v.5), interpretation (v.25), death (v.30), new empire (v.31). Five scenes in thirty-one verses.

collins.source: John J. Collins, Daniel, Hermeneia (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Collins notes the wordplay is unique in ancient literature: a cryptic inscription using commercial terms that double as prophetic verdicts. The writing functions like an oracle but is delivered in accounting language.

Collins observes that the shift from Babylonian to Medo-Persian rule in one night confirms the statue’s prediction (ch.2): the gold head gives way to the silver chest. The prophecy self-verifies within the narrative.

hist.context: The three Aramaic words are also units of weight/currency: mina, shekel, half-mina. The pun works on two levels: (1) monetary terms (your kingdom has been measured like currency) and (2) verbal roots (numbered, weighed, divided). The wordplay on PARSIN/Persia (prs) adds a third level: your kingdom goes to Persia. The Babylonian Chronicle records Cyrus’s conquest of Babylon on October 12, 539 BC — the city fell without a battle, consistent with the feast-night surprise.

ppl.name: Belshazzar
ppl.text: Co-regent with Nabonidus. His feast desecrating the temple vessels provokes the writing on the wall. Slain the same night.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe king summoned the enchanters, astrologers and diviners. Then he said to these wise men of Babylon, \u201cWhoever reads this writing and tells me what it means will be clothed in purple and have a gold chain placed around his neck, and he will be made the third highest ruler in the kingdom.\u201dESVThe king called loudly to bring in the enchanters, the Chaldeans, and the astrologers. The king declared to the wise men of Babylon, \u201cWhoever reads this writing, and shows me its interpretation, shall be clothed with purple and have a chain of gold around his neck and shall be the third ruler in the kingdom.\u201dNIVHis face turned pale and he was so frightened that his legs became weak and his knees were knocking.ESVThen the king\u2019s color changed, and his thoughts alarmed him; his limbs gave way, and his knees knocked together.

src.title: The Prayer of Nabonidus (4Q242, Dead Sea Scrolls)
src.quote: A fragmentary Qumran text describes a Babylonian king struck with illness for seven years who is healed by a Jewish exorcist — strikingly parallel to Nebuchadnezzar’s madness in Daniel 4.
src.note: The Prayer of Nabonidus may preserve an older version of the tradition behind Daniel 4, or reflect a common Near Eastern motif of royal humbling. Either way, it confirms the antiquity of the Daniel traditions.

src.title: 1 Maccabees 1–2 (2nd c. BC)
src.quote: Describes the historical desecration of the Jerusalem temple by Antiochus IV Epiphanes in 167 BC — the event Daniel 8:9–14 and 11:31 prophesy as the “abomination of desolation.”
src.note: The fulfilment is documented in non-biblical Jewish sources, confirming Daniel’s prophetic precision whether the prophecy is dated to the 6th or 2nd century BC.

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities 10.11 (1st c. AD)
src.quote: Josephus records that when Alexander the Great approached Jerusalem (332 BC), the high priest showed him Daniel’s prophecy about a Greek king conquering Persia, and Alexander was so impressed he offered sacrifices at the temple.
src.note: Whether historically accurate or legendary, Josephus’s account shows that Daniel’s prophecies were understood as referring to Greek conquest well before the Christian era.

rec.text: "Belshazzar’s feast. Sacred vessels desecrated. The sacrilege is deliberate and public."

rows.label: Numbered, Weighed, Divided — 5:17–31
rows.text: MENE MENE TEKEL PARSIN. "Weighed and found WANTING." That very night the king was slain.

hebtext.note: "Suddenly the fingers of a human hand appeared and wrote (kəṭab) on the plaster of the wall" (v.5). Belshazzar’s feast: a thousand nobles, drinking from the sacred vessels taken from Jerusalem’s temple. A hand writes. The king’s face turns pale, his knees knock. No wise man can read it. The queen remembers Daniel.

"MENE, MENE, TEKEL, PARSIN" (v.25). Daniel recounts Nebuchadnezzar’s humbling then rebukes Belshazzar: "You knew all this, yet you have not humbled yourself" (vv.22–23). MENE — numbered. TEKEL — weighed and found wanting. PARSIN — divided and given to Medes and Persians. That very night Belshazzar is slain (v.30). Darius the Mede takes over (v.31).

thread.text: Cyrus’s decree fulfilling Jeremiah’s prophecy — the Persian conquest that Daniel 5 narrates from the Babylonian side.
Babylon the great, drunk with the blood of the saints — Revelation’s Babylon draws on the feast imagery of Daniel 5.
In the hand of the LORD is a cup full of foaming wine — the cup of judgment Belshazzar drinks from literally.
Jeremiah’s prophecy of 70 years of Babylonian dominion — fulfilled at the moment Daniel 5 describes.
"Fallen, fallen is Babylon!" — the double-fallen formula that Daniel 5 narrates.
In one hour your judgment has come — Revelation’s Babylon falls as suddenly as Daniel’s.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10: the swiftest divine verdict in the OT. Sovereignty at 9: God numbers, weighs, divides kingdoms.

coaching.tip: Belshazzar drinks from the Jerusalem temple vessels at his feast (v. 3) — a deliberate desecration. The hand writes on the wall while he holds sacred cups. The timing is the message: sacrilege triggers judgment instantly. Daniel 5 compresses the gap between sin and consequence to a single evening. The chapter reads like a thriller because it is one — Babylon falls before dawn.

'TEKEL — you have been weighed on the scales and found wanting' (v. 27). The image of divine scales appears throughout ancient Near Eastern religion, but Daniel's version is unique: the judge is the God of Israel, and the one weighed is the most powerful king on earth. Power doesn't exempt you from evaluation — it intensifies it. Belshazzar's grandfather Nebuchadnezzar learned humility (chapter 4); Belshazzar 'did not humble himself, though you knew all this' (v. 22). Knowledge without response is the heaviest charge.
